questions.questionText: A 25-year-old man hears voices arguing about him (third-person auditory hallucinations), believes the TV is sending him special messages (delusion of reference), and says his thoughts are being broadcast to others. These symptoms are classified as:
questions.optionB: First-rank symptoms of Schneider (Schneiderian first-rank symptoms — considered highly suggestive but not pathognomonic of schizophrenia)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Schneider's first-rank symptoms include auditory hallucinations (3rd person, running commentary, thought echo), thought insertion/withdrawal/broadcasting, passivity phenomena, and delusional perception. Why correct: Third-person hallucinations + thought broadcast + delusions of reference are all Schneiderian first-rank symptoms. Present in ~60-70% of schizophrenia. Why A wrong: Negative symptoms: anhedonia, alogia, avolition, affective flattening, asociality. Why C wrong: Second-rank symptoms: other hallucinations, perplexity, emotional blunting. Why D wrong: Prodromal (pre-onset): social withdrawal, odd behavior, decreased functioning — non-specific. Clinical relevance: ICD-11 has moved away from Schneiderian emphasis but they remain exam-relevant. Bleuler's 4 A's: Ambivalence, Autism, Associations (loosening), Affect (inappropriate). Exam trap: First-rank symptoms are NOT pathognomonic — can occur in mania, temporal lobe epilepsy, and substance use. Memory aid: 'First Rank = FRS = Foreign thoughts (broadcast/insert/withdraw) + Running commentary + Special messages.' Reference: Ahuja – Schizophrenia.

questions.questionText: The most common type of hallucination in schizophrenia is:
questions.optionB: Auditory (voices — heard by ~70% of schizophrenia patients; visual hallucinations are more suggestive of organic/drug-related causes)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Auditory hallucinations are the hallmark of schizophrenia; visual suggest organic etiology. Why correct: Auditory hallucinations occur in ~70% of schizophrenia. Types: voices commenting, commanding, conversing, thought echo. Why A wrong: Visual hallucinations more common in organic psychosis, delirium, substance use (LSD, alcohol withdrawal), Charles Bonnet syndrome. Why C wrong: Olfactory (uncinate hallucinations) — temporal lobe epilepsy, occasionally schizophrenia. Why D wrong: Tactile — cocaine ('cocaine bugs'/formication), delirium tremens, dermatological conditions. Clinical relevance: If visual hallucinations are prominent → rule out organic/substance cause FIRST (organic psychosis more likely). Exam trap: Auditory = schizophrenia (functional); Visual = organic/drug-induced. This is a FUNDAMENTAL distinction. Memory aid: 'Schizo = Sounds (auditory); Organic = Optical (visual).' Reference: Ahuja – Schizophrenia.

questions.questionText: A patient on haloperidol develops sustained involuntary contraction causing twisting of the neck (torticollis) within hours of starting the medication. This is:
questions.optionB: Acute dystonia (earliest EPS — occurs within hours to days of starting typical antipsychotics; treat with IV anticholinergics: diphenhydramine/benztropine)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Acute dystonia = earliest EPS, sustained muscle contractions, treat with anticholinergics. Why correct: Acute dystonia: hours-days after antipsychotic initiation. Types: torticollis (neck), oculogyric crisis (eyes), trismus (jaw), opisthotonus. Young males most susceptible. Treat: IV diphenhydramine or benztropine (anticholinergics). Why A wrong: Tardive dyskinesia = LATE onset (months-years), choreiform movements of face/tongue (lip-smacking, tongue protrusion). Due to dopamine receptor hypersensitivity. Worsened by anticholinergics. Why C wrong: Akathisia = subjective restlessness, inability to sit still. Days-weeks after starting. Treat: beta-blockers (propranolol) or benzodiazepines. Why D wrong: Parkinsonism = weeks-months, bradykinesia/rigidity/tremor. Treat: anticholinergics. Clinical relevance: EPS timeline: dystonia (hours) → akathisia (days) → parkinsonism (weeks) → tardive dyskinesia (months-years). Exam trap: Tardive dyskinesia is WORSENED by anticholinergics (opposite of other EPS). Treatment: valbenazine, deutetrabenazine, or switch to clozapine. Memory aid: 'DAP-T timeline: Dystonia-Akathisia-Parkinsonism-Tardive (chronological EPS).' Reference: Ahuja – Antipsychotics.
questions.conceptTag: Acute dystonia EPS haloperidol anticholinergic treatment
